arguments.query: PlayHT API emotion control parameter documentation voice style

queries.query: PlayHT API emotion control parameter documentation voice style
queries.answer:
The PlayHT API offers advanced control over speech synthesis, including the ability to adjust emotional tone and voice style. According to the documentation, there is an "Emotion Control" parameter that allows users to generate speech with specific emotions and tones to match content requirements ([api-docs.ollang.com](https://api-docs.ollang.com/apis/tts-apis/playht)). Additionally, the API supports a "voice style" parameter, which can influence the overall delivery and character of the synthesized voice, although specific details about the range of styles are not explicitly provided in the search results.

Furthermore, the API provides parameters to customize the output, such as voice engine selection, language, speed, pitch, and output format. For real-time applications, the API supports streaming audio generation, which can be configured with various parameters to match desired emotional and stylistic effects ([play.ht/reference/api-generate-tts-audio-stream](https://docs.play.ht/reference/api-generate-tts-audio-stream)). The "emotion control" parameter typically accepts numerical values where lower numbers produce less emotional speech, and higher numbers create more expressive, emotional performances ([play.ht/reference/api-generate-tts-audio-stream](https://docs.play.ht/reference/api-generate-tts-audio-stream)). 

In summary, the PlayHT API provides parameters for emotion and style control to tailor the voice output, with the emotion parameter allowing for nuanced emotional expression, and voice style influencing the overall tone and character of the speech ([api-docs.ollang.com](https://api-docs.ollang.com/apis/tts-apis/playht)).

content.text:
# PlayHT API Emotion & Style Control Summary

## Key Findings

The PlayHT API includes dedicated parameters for controlling emotional tone and voice style in synthesized speech:

**Emotion Control Parameter:**
- Accepts numerical values to adjust expressiveness
- Lower values produce less emotional speech
- Higher values create more expressive, emotional performances
- Allows fine-tuned emotional expression matching content requirements

**Voice Style Parameter:**
- Influences overall delivery and character of the synthesized voice
- Specific range of available styles not explicitly detailed in available documentation

## Additional Customization Options

Beyond emotion and style, the API supports:
- Voice engine selection
- Language configuration
- Speech speed and pitch adjustment
- Output format specification
- Real-time streaming audio generation with configurable parameters

## Caveats

- The search results reference emotion control but provide limited detail on exact acceptable ranges or predefined emotion categories
- Voice style options are mentioned but specific available styles are not enumerated in the provided documentation excerpts
- Detailed implementation examples are not included in these results

## Sources

- https://docs.play.ht/reference/api-generate-tts-audio-stream
- https://api-docs.ollang.com/apis/tts-apis/playht
- https://docs.play.ht/reference

The PlayHT API offers advanced control over speech synthesis, including the ability to adjust emotional tone and voice style. According to the documentation, there is an "Emotion Control" parameter that allows users to generate speech with specific emotions and tones to match content requirements ([api-docs.ollang.com](https://api-docs.ollang.com/apis/tts-apis/playht)). Additionally, the API supports a "voice style" parameter, which can influence the overall delivery and character of the synthesized voice, although specific details about the range of styles are not explicitly provided in the search results.

Furthermore, the API provides parameters to customize the output, such as voice engine selection, language, speed, pitch, and output format. For real-time applications, the API supports streaming audio generation, which can be configured with various parameters to match desired emotional and stylistic effects ([play.ht/reference/api-generate-tts-audio-stream](https://docs.play.ht/reference/api-generate-tts-audio-stream)). The "emotion control" parameter typically accepts numerical values where lower numbers produce less emotional speech, and higher numbers create more expressive, emotional performances ([play.ht/reference/api-generate-tts-audio-stream](https://docs.play.ht/reference/api-generate-tts-audio-stream)). 

In summary, the PlayHT API provides parameters for emotion and style control to tailor the voice output, with the emotion parameter allowing for nuanced emotional expression, and voice style influencing the overall tone and character of the speech ([api-docs.ollang.com](https://api-docs.ollang.com/apis/tts-apis/playht)).

summary.text:
# PlayHT API Emotion & Style Control Summary

## Key Findings

The PlayHT API includes dedicated parameters for controlling emotional tone and voice style in synthesized speech:

**Emotion Control Parameter:**
- Accepts numerical values to adjust expressiveness
- Lower values produce less emotional speech
- Higher values create more expressive, emotional performances
- Allows fine-tuned emotional expression matching content requirements

**Voice Style Parameter:**
- Influences overall delivery and character of the synthesized voice
- Specific range of available styles not explicitly detailed in available documentation

## Additional Customization Options

Beyond emotion and style, the API supports:
- Voice engine selection
- Language configuration
- Speech speed and pitch adjustment
- Output format specification
- Real-time streaming audio generation with configurable parameters

## Caveats

- The search results reference emotion control but provide limited detail on exact acceptable ranges or predefined emotion categories
- Voice style options are mentioned but specific available styles are not enumerated in the provided documentation excerpts
- Detailed implementation examples are not included in these results

## Sources

- https://docs.play.ht/reference/api-generate-tts-audio-stream
- https://api-docs.ollang.com/apis/tts-apis/playht
- https://docs.play.ht/reference

content.text:
Here's the real landscape:

| Provider | Personality Control | Cost/1M chars | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unreal Speech** (current) | Speed/Pitch only | ~$0.01 | No room for personality |
| **OpenAI TTS** | Natural language `instructions` | ~$15 | Sweet spot |
| **PlayHT 3.0** | Numeric emotion presets | ~$15-30 est. | OK, less flexible |
| **Deepgram Aura** | None | ~$27 | Worse than Unreal, no control |
| **Hume AI** | `description` acting cue | ~$150 | Good control, way too pricey |
| **ElevenLabs** | Full control | ~$150+ | Best quality, 10x OpenAI |

There's no "OpenAI-quality personality control at Unreal Speech prices." The personality steering requires a smarter model, which costs more.

**OpenAI is the practical sweet spot for pi-speak.** The `instructions` parameter maps perfectly to a `personality` config field — you'd write natural language like `"Dry, wry tone. Like a colleague leaning over."` and it consistently delivers. At $15/M chars, even heavy usage would be pennies per day for short pings + occasional full readback.

The question is really: do you want to add OpenAI as an alternative TTS backend, or outright replace Unreal Speech?

content.text:
All green — 51 tests, lint, format, type check. Here's what changed:

### New files

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `src/tts-provider.ts` | `TTSProvider` interface + `SynthesisResult`, `BaseTTSConfig` types |
| `src/tts-unreal.ts` | `UnrealSpeechProvider` — extracted from old `tts.ts` |
| `src/tts-openai.ts` | `OpenAITTSProvider` — new, uses `gpt-4o-mini-tts` with `instructions` |

### Modified files

- **`src/tts.ts`** — De-coupled from Unreal Speech. Now takes a `TTSProvider` and delegates synthesis. No provider-specific code remains.
- **`src/config/v1/schema.ts`** — `ReadbackConfigSchema` is now a discriminated union on `provider: "unreal" | "openai"`. Each has its own voice/settings shape.
- **`src/config/index.ts`** — Debug log handles both providers via type narrowing.
- **`src/index.ts`** — Creates the correct provider based on `config.readback.provider`.
- **`src/tts.test.ts`** — Rewritten to test via mocked providers instead of mocking the Unreal Speech API.

### Config shape (OpenAI)

```json
{
  "readback": {
    "provider": "openai",
    "voice": "coral",
    "model": "gpt-4o-mini-tts",
    "instructions": "Dry, slightly amused tone. Like a colleague leaning over to tell you what just happened. Casual, warm, never theatrical.",
    "speed": 1,
    "format": "mp3",
    "maxChunkChars": 1000
  }
}
```

The `instructions` field is the personality lever — natural language. Voice options: `alloy`, `ash`, `ballad`, `coral`, `echo`, `fable`, `onyx`, `nova`, `sage`, `shimmer`, `verse`, `marin`, `cedar`.

Existing configs without a `provider` field will fall through to Unreal Speech (no breaking change — `loadConfig` handles the migration path).

Two problems:

2. **You haven't restarted the Pi session yet** — the last `session_start` in the debug log predates our changes, so Unreal Speech is still running from the old code.

Here's what to do:

**To stick with Unreal Speech for now** — create a config file:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.config/pi-speak && echo '{"readback":{"provider":"unreal"}}' > ~/.config/pi-speak/config.json
```

content.text:
# OpenRouter Text-to-Speech API Summary

## Key Findings

**Endpoint & Availability**
- OpenRouter provides a `/api/v1/audio/speech` endpoint for text-to-speech functionality
- The GPT-4o Mini TTS model is supported and accessible through OpenRouter
- The service accepts text input and returns raw audio streams

**Model Details**
- **GPT-4o Mini TTS** is described as a cost-efficient, natural-sounding TTS model
- Supports various voices and tones
- Recent model version available: `gpt-4o-mini-tts-2025-12-15`

**SDK Support**
- OpenRouter provides official SDK support for TTS across multiple languages:
  - Python
  - TypeScript
  - Go

## Caveats
- Search results do not include specific pricing details or rate limits
- Feature request history (GitHub issue #15726) suggests TTS provider support was recently added, but current implementation status is not explicitly confirmed in these results

---

## Sources

- [OpenRouter TTS Documentation](https://openrouter.ai/docs/guides/overview/multimodal/tts)
- [GPT-4o Mini TTS Model Page](https://openrouter.ai/openai/gpt-4o-mini-tts-2025-12-15)
- [TTS API Reference](https://openrouter.ai/docs/api/api-reference/tts/create-tts)

# OpenRouter Text-to-Speech API Summary

## Key Findings

**Endpoint & Availability**
- OpenRouter provides a `/api/v1/audio/speech` endpoint for text-to-speech functionality
- The GPT-4o Mini TTS model is supported and accessible through OpenRouter
- The service accepts text input and returns raw audio streams

**Model Details**
- **GPT-4o Mini TTS** is described as a cost-efficient, natural-sounding TTS model
- Supports various voices and tones
- Recent model version available: `gpt-4o-mini-tts-2025-12-15`

**SDK Support**
- OpenRouter provides official SDK support for TTS across multiple languages:
  - Python
  - TypeScript
  - Go

## Caveats
- Search results do not include specific pricing details or rate limits
- Feature request history (GitHub issue #15726) suggests TTS provider support was recently added, but current implementation status is not explicitly confirmed in these results

---

- [OpenRouter TTS Documentation](https://openrouter.ai/docs/guides/overview/multimodal/tts)
- [GPT-4o Mini TTS Model Page](https://openrouter.ai/openai/gpt-4o-mini-tts-2025-12-15)
- [TTS API Reference](https://openrouter.ai/docs/api/api-reference/tts/create-tts)

urls.title: OpenRouter Text-to-Speech - Complete Documentation
urls.content:
How to generate speech audio from text with OpenRouter models

OpenRouter supports text-to-speech (TTS) via a dedicated `/api/v1/audio/speech` endpoint that is compatible with the [OpenAI Audio Speech API](https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference/audio/createSpeech). Send text and receive a raw audio byte stream in your chosen format.

## Model Discovery

You can find TTS models in several ways:

### Via the API

Use the `output_modalities` query parameter on the [Models API](/docs/api-reference/models/get-models) to discover TTS models:

### On the Models Page

Visit the [Models page](/docs/models) and filter by output modalities to find models capable of speech synthesis. Look for models that list `"speech"` in their output modalities.

## API Usage

Send a `POST` request to `/api/v1/audio/speech` with the text you want to synthesize. The response is a raw audio byte stream — not JSON — so you can pipe it directly to a file or audio player.

### Basic Example

### Request Parameters

### Provider-Specific Options

You can pass provider-specific options using the `provider` parameter. Options are keyed by provider slug, and only the options for the matched provider are forwarded:

## Response Format

The TTS endpoint returns a **raw audio byte stream**, not JSON. The response includes the following headers:

### Output Formats

## Pricing

TTS models are priced **per character** of input text. Pricing varies by model and provider. You can check the per-character cost for each model on the [Models page](/docs/models) or via the [Models API](/docs/api-reference/models/get-models).

## OpenAI SDK Compatibility

The TTS endpoint is fully compatible with the OpenAI SDK. You can use the OpenAI client libraries by pointing them at OpenRouter’s base URL:

## Best Practices

*   **Choose the right format**: Use `mp3` for storage and general playback. Use `pcm` for real-time streaming pipelines where latency matters
*   **Voice selection**: Different providers offer different voices. Check the model’s documentation or experiment with available voices to find the best fit for your use case
*   **Input length**: For very long texts, consider splitting the input into smaller segments and concatenating the audio output. This can improve reliability and reduce latency for the first audio chunk
*   **Speed parameter**: The `speed` parameter is only supported by certain providers (e.g., OpenAI). It is silently ignored by providers that don’t support it

## Troubleshooting

**Empty or corrupted audio file?**

*   Verify the `response_format` matches how you’re saving the file (e.g., don’t save `pcm` output with a `.mp3` extension)
*   Check the response status code — non-200 responses return JSON error bodies, not audio

**Model not found?**

*   Use the [Models page](/docs/models) to find available TTS models
*   Verify the model slug is correct (e.g., `openai/gpt-4o-mini-tts-2025-12-15`, not `gpt-4o-mini-tts`)

**Voice not available?**

*   Available voices vary by provider. Check the provider’s documentation for supported voice identifiers
*   Each model has its own set of voices — check the model’s page on the [Models page](/docs/models) for the full list

content.text:
# OpenRouter GPT-4o Mini TTS Model ID

## Key Finding
The OpenRouter model ID for GPT-4o Mini TTS is:
```
openai/gpt-4o-mini-tts-2025-12-15
```

This is the exact slug needed to access the model through OpenRouter's API.

## Context
- GPT-4o Mini TTS is OpenAI's text-to-speech model available via OpenRouter
- OpenRouter provides pricing and API documentation for this model
- The model identifier includes a date stamp (2025-12-15), indicating a specific version

## Sources
- [OpenRouter GPT-4o Mini TTS Pricing & Providers](https://openrouter.ai/openai/gpt-4o-mini-tts-2025-12-15)
- [OpenRouter TTS Documentation](https://openrouter.ai/docs/guides/overview/multimodal/tts)

content.thinking:
I can see two things:

1. The first error (13:50:36) at `ping: failed: OpenAITTSProvider not initialized` — this happened because the provider's `initialize()` method wasn't called. Looking at the code, the `TTSPlayer` doesn't call `provider.initialize()` anywhere. The `session_start` checks if the API key exists but doesn't call `initialize()` on the provider.

2. After the restart (13:53:19), the config loads correctly: `provider=openai voice=coral model=openai/gpt-4o-mini-tts-2025-12-15`. The session_start says "API key loaded, extension enabled". But the provider still isn't initialized — `initialize()` is never called.

The bug: `TTSPlayer` (and `index.ts`) never calls `provider.initialize()`. The `OpenAITTSProvider.initialize()` method checks for the API key and stores it, but the `synthesize` method checks `if (!this.apiKey) throw new Error("OpenAITTSProvider not initialized")`.

In `index.ts`, the session_start handler checks the API key in the env, but doesn't call `provider.initialize()`. The provider is created, but never initialized.

Fix: I need to either:
1. Call `provider.initialize()` in `TTSPlayer` constructor or before `synthesize`
2. Remove the `initialize()` concept and have the provider check the key lazily
3. Call `provider.initialize()` in `index.ts` during session_start

Option 3 is the cleanest — call `provider.initialize()` in `index.ts` during session_start, and if it fails, disable the extension.

content.text:
Found the bug. The provider is never initialized — `initialize()` is never called. The session says "API key loaded" but the provider's `synthesize()` throws because `this.apiKey` is null.

Fix: call `provider.initialize()` during session start, and disable on failure.

2. **Your config is set up** at `~/.config/pi-speak/config.json` — OpenRouter, coral voice, `openai/gpt-4o-mini-tts-2025-12-15`.

Restart Pi and you should hear the OpenAI voice through OpenRouter. Watch `~/.pi-speak-debug.log` — you'll see `session_start: provider initialized successfully` if it works.

content:
IF A SKILL APPLIES TO YOUR TASK, YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE. YOU MUST USE IT.

This is not negotiable. This is not optional. You cannot rationalize your way out of this.

If you think there is even a 1% chance a skill might apply, you ABSOLUTELY MUST invoke the skill using the Skill tool BEFORE any response, any action, any clarifying question.

User instructions say WHAT, not HOW. "Add X" or "Fix Y" does not mean skip workflows. Check for skills FIRST.

## Priority Chain

1. **User's explicit instructions** (CLAUDE.md, direct requests) — highest priority
2. **Skills** — override default system behavior where they conflict
3. **Default system prompt** — lowest priority

If CLAUDE.md contradicts a skill, follow CLAUDE.md. The user is in control.

## Anti-Rationalization

These thoughts mean STOP — you are rationalizing skipping a skill check:

| Thought                              | Reality                                                                        |
| ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| "This is simple enough"              | Simple tasks have skills too. Check.                                           |
| "I already know how"                 | Skills contain project-specific overrides you don't have memorized. Invoke it. |
| "Let me explore first"               | Skills tell you HOW to explore. Check first.                                   |
| "I need more context"                | Skill check comes BEFORE clarifying questions. Always.                         |
| "The skill is overkill"              | Simple things become complex. Use it.                                          |
| "I'll just do this one thing first"  | Check BEFORE doing anything. No exceptions.                                    |
| "I remember this skill"              | Skills evolve. Read the current version via the Skill tool.                    |
| "This doesn't need a formal process" | If a skill exists for it, use it. Period.                                      |
| "Let me look at the code quickly"    | Files lack conversation context. Check for skills.                             |
| "This feels productive"              | Undisciplined action wastes time. Skills prevent this.                         |
| "This is just a discussion"          | Questions about features, bugs, or architecture ARE development tasks. Check.  |

## Skill Types

**Rigid** (TDD, commit format, verification): Follow exactly. Do not adapt away discipline.

**Flexible** (React patterns, styling, architecture): Adapt principles to context. The spirit matters more than the letter.

The skill itself tells you which type it is. When unclear, treat as rigid.

## Skill Priority

When multiple skills apply:

1. **Process skills first** (brainstorm, start, planning, debugging) — these determine HOW to approach the task
2. **Domain skills second** (react, typescript, tanstack) — these guide execution
3. **Workflow skills third** (commit, verification, close) — these govern shipping

## Subagent Exemption

If you were dispatched as a subagent to execute a specific, scoped task — skip this enforcement. Subagents follow their prompt, not the full skill ceremony.
